Issue No.1 9 • February 20 I 3
How To Make Out Like A Bandit
Doing Special Sales With Email
What up Email Player,
Today I am going to hand you the proverbial "cash machine" all the goo-roo ads
promise, but can never quite deliver on. All you need is (1) email (duh, right?) (2) a
product to sell (yours or someone else's, doesn't matter) and (3) an irresistible offer. I think
you will agree, all 3 of these ingredients are readily available to anyone and won't "cost"
you anything to acquire in most cases. And what we're going to do is show you how to
leverage these 3 components into a predictable and repeatable (i.e. you can do this over
and over and over... forever) process that pays you year in, and year out for the rest of
your days. To illustrate what I'm talking about I'm going to walk you through something I
do every year (and will KEEP doing every year) on Labor Day weekend here in the US. I
this with other products (as an affiliate and for my Copywriting Grab Bag product), too.
But I'm gonna focus just on this one product today to keep it simple. It also helps to use
what I'm about to show you in conjunction with a holiday weekend, but ifs not I 00%
mandatory. You could just as easily do this any time of the year. So no need to get hung
up on that part. What's important are the 3 components above. Gather them together
and then come sit at my table and I'll explain how to make a nice chunk of change while,
at the same time, making your customers very (VERY) happy.
Sound good to you, boss? Then let's get. it. aaaaawn ...

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
Okay so what rm going to show you is not ALL that different than the process I
use to launch new products (that we talked about in the December, 2011 "Email Players"
issue). In fact, much of it overlaps. However, since we're not launching a new product, but
simply selling an existing product there are some "cosmetic" differences. And don't worry,
I'll be showing you ALL the emails I use in my yearly "money machine" sale that, last
year, got my JV partner and I a 30.8% clickthrough to sales response. (And that response
goes up each year, even though many of the previous years' customers are still on my list.)
That way, you can use them as inspiration and, in many (not all) cases ~ike with the
blatant reminder emails) "as is"-- just swapping out my product info with yours. (You'll
have to tell your own stories, though, of course, in those emails). My amigo, I just cannot
make it any easier than this.
Let's boogie...
Step 1:
Pick the right product
No kidding, right? Well, this isn't as obvious as it seems at first. I would, for
example, not do this kind of sale with this newsletter. This special sale is not necessarily
appropriate for every product. I will do it for my Copywriting Grab Bag product
sometimes. But mostly, I do this for products I am an affiliate for. That way I don't have to
deal with any of the customer service, product delivery, questions or anything else. I
recommend you do the same. Find a product your list will REALLY want that someone
else sells. In this case, I sell Michael Senoff's Gene Schwartz copywriting product. It's
extremely valuable, gets rave reviews from everyone who buys it and (as you will see) I
have a personal story to talk about with it which will be important. In fact, whatever
product you pick, make sure it's one you've used and had success with. It will make a huge
difference in your first email to your list and sets the "tone" for the rest of the sale that
you're not just pitching more of the "same old, same old" on them. You can use your own
product, if you want. But my preference is to do it with other peoples' products as an
affiliate purely for convenience reasons.
Step 2:
Create An Irresistible Offer
Sounds almost like "marketing l 0 l ", doesn't it?

-- 2 of 34 --

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
Hey, it IS marketing 101 -- but don't worry, the cool stuff will show in a minute
when this all comes together. Anyway, this is extremely important. You MUST put
together the best offer you can possibly arrange. In my case, Michael and I agree each
year to sell his Gene Schwartz copywriting product to my list for a measly $20 when it
normally sells for $297. That's one hellz of a discount. And makes it almost impossible for
someone interested in the subject to resist buying. Plus, I take it a step further than that. I
also give away another unique bonus (that I control) called the "Sales Letter Jam Session"
which is this really cool round table interview I did with a couple of my droogs about the
process of writing an ad (people who write ads are often very interested in others'
processes). To make this simple, I arrange it with Michael so he puts my bonus on the
download page with the main product. If this wasn't practical for some reason, I'd have
my list send me their PayPal receipt and then manually send them the unique bonus. But
I cannot see how this would be a problem with any publisher to deliver your bonus for
you.
Anyway, so make your offer IRRESISTIBLE.
In my case, I do it two ways:
A gigantic discount (over 90% discount) on a valuable product, and I also toss in a
unique bonus. If I was really ambitious about this, I'd have several unique bonuses. And
maybe next year I'll experiment with that. But for now, just really put a lot of thought
into an irresistible offer. It's so important, yet most people never put much thought into it
for some reason.
Step 3:
Bulld A Sublist
Now we're getting to good stuff. This step is one most people are way too lazy to
do. Or, they simply don't think it will be worth the 30 minutes of effort. But having a
sublist of people can make all the difference. When I do my sale, I mail TWO lists: My
main list at BenSettle.com (a mix of people who are interested in email marketing,
copywriting, web marketing, etc, but not all necessarily in to copywriting) and my sublist
of people who are SPECIFICALLY interested in copywriting I build at
CopywritingGrabBag.com. And I mail both lists simultaneously during the sale -- but with
different emails and at different times (due to overlap of people on both lists).

-- 3 of 34 --

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
If you don't already have a sublist of people interested in the kind of product you
want to do a sale for, start building one ASAP.
Today.
A fast way to do it is to throw up a squeeze page telling them they will get
specialized tips about whatever the topic is, promise a great bribe, and then load that
auto-responder with emails you've already written on the topic. Maybe space them 3 days
apart or whatever depending on how many you have. The list at
CopywritingGrabBag.com is a year's worth of emails dripped out 3 days apart. Now, you
don't have to do this. You can also just tell them they'll be getting whatever irresistible
offer you are giving them on the squeeze page, and to give you their email to be notified.
But I like to have a pre-loaded sequence so I can make sales even after the sale is over and
keep the leads "warmed up" so I can do another sale (for another product) later in the
year. You can build this list by just telling your main list about it every now and again. You
can also let the search engines do their thing over time, or generate other traffic for it if
you want. In my case, I let people find it on their own, but also tell my main list about it
every now and then so there's always a trickle of people (all year) joining it.
Step 4:
Load Up Both List's Auto-responders
With A Thursday-Sunday Entail Sequence
And now we get to the last step. And, the step with all the manual labor. But
remember, once you set this up once, you can RE-USE it over and over and over again --
for years and years, with little or no actual changes to anything. So it's like a little money
machine you turn on once or twice per year (or however often you want to do it, but I
would not recommend ever burning your lists out). All the emails I use are in the bonus
report that come with this issue. But I'm going to do a bit of 'splaining so you realize the
strategy behind everything. So, first thing is, I do not recommend mailing the same emails
to both your main list and sublist. Again, there's going to be a ton of overlap on the lists
and you don't want to piss them off by lazily sending them the same emails. The power in
the sublist is those people are especially interested in the subject you are writing about. So
you can get more aggressive with them tl1an with your main list and not have much (if
any, I didn't get a single complaint the last two times I did this kind of sale) fallout.

-- 4 of 34 --

THE 	EMAIL PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
What I do is this:
1.) Mail my main listl email per day, Thursday through
Saturday, and send 4 emails on Sunday Qeading up to the
deadline-- which I set as midnight eastern time)
2.) Mail my sublist 2 emails per dayThursday-Saturday, and
send 5-7 emails on Sunday. (Again, you can get more
aggressive with this list.)
The exact sublist emails are enclosed with this newsletter. You can easily model
and adapt the sublist emails (which are mostly boilerplate-friendly) however you want. But
I want to give a bit of strategy behind them so you understand WHY they are written a
certain way, and why you should not veer too far away from the overall structures of the
first and last day emails.
OK that said, here are a couple things I recommend:
For your MAIN list I highly suggest you begin the sale with a personal story about
how the product benefited you. This will not only nab you lots of sales (especially if you
nail the offer and product right}, but sets a nice "tone" for the rest of the sale. It's not just
perceived as you trying to line your pockets. It's perceived as more of a MISSION you're
on. A mission of "here's how this product solved my XYZ problem, and I REALLY want
you to have it, thus I've arranged this awesome offer for you ... " It's a different tone and
feel to it than the usual self-interest centered sale. I also recommend (as you will see in the
sample emails) you don't get real blatant to your main list. Make the emails like you would
otherwise (fun, interesting, infotaining, Q&A, all the stuff taught in the "Email Players
Cookbook" you got with your subscription to this newsletter), but segue strongly into
talking about the sale in each email. Remember, if you pick the right product and create
the right irresistible offer, you are not imposing on your list. You are doing them a
FAVOR and trust me, many will even thank you for doing the sale.
Now, to your sublist, I recommend you be more blatant.
Tell them you are having a sale!
Yes, even in the subject line.

-- 5 of 34 --

THE 	EMAIL PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
In fact, email grand puba Matt Furey once said (and I agree with this
wholeheartedly) that just putting the word "sale" in the subject line is guaranteed to get it
opened by lots of people who usually ignore your emails. Make sure you tuck that little
gem away for future use (don't overdo it though, MF also says a strength overextended is
no longer a strength -- and that includes constantly reusing the same subject lines too
often). And remember how it says 2 emails per day for the sublist? Well, that means on
day 1 they are both short (the first saying, "hey I'm having a sale!" and the second one at
night saying, 'just a reminder about the sale... "). Then, the next couple days (Friday and
Saturday) you write an email like you would normally early, and at night another blatant
reminder about the sale email.
On the last day you do the infamous Senoff "countdown" sequence.
Michael is the first one I know of to do this, and it's brilliant in its simplicity.
This is where you create an almost "eBay-like" affect by reminding them
throughout the day tl1e sale is ending at midnight. I like to mix these up a bit, some
content-looking, others blatant reminders. Again, you can see the enclosed emails for
examples. I have done so many of these kinds of sales (for various markets) that I have it
down to almost a science. Thus, I would not recommend veering too far off from what I
do your first time. By all means experiment and adapt, but just realize I've done literally
dozens of these sales.
And that's all there is to it.
Pretty simple stuff, Boss.
Plus, you can do these sales all year, too, if you want. The example enclosed with
this newsletter is every Labor Day. But, for example, in weight loss, we would do 6 sales
per year -- for the major holidays. Doing this let us make a LOT more sales than normal,
especially during the Fall-Winter when that market is usually dead. (The Thanksgiving
sale was an especially big hit). However often you do them, realize when you do these
right, you can reliably expect to make more and more sales each year (as you tweak and
adapt and add more names to your list). Frankly, after you do this the first time you'll
probably go nutz thinking about how much MORE money you would have made if you
just had a bigger list.

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
You Don't Want To Write
''Asshole Em.ails''?
Then Do This ...
Last month I wrote about the power of writing what I call "asshole emails"
whenever you get a sudden surge of new subscribers. The point of which is to drive away
people who are not ever going to make good customers, are never going to buy and, in
fact, will likely complain, nag and accuse you of "spam" or whatever. Plus, even if they
DO buy, there's a very big chance they'll be nightmare customers who suck up all your
time, energy and patience and who needs that? So what I said was to ramp up your
asshole emails (not being insulting or anything, but just edgy, controversial, etc). Well,
some of y'all aren't comfY with that. Some even are in markets where that sort of thing
probably would not be the best course of action. And some are just too chicken (ha). So, if
you fall into any of the above, fear ye not. Instead of writing asshole emails when you get
a sudden surge of new subscribers, then another thing you can do is simply do a few
blatant pitch emails the first 5 or 6 days they are on your list. Your main list will likely
tolerate it, and the newer ones will either be offended and flee like a vampire from a silver
cross, or they may end up BUYING. Either way, you win. The key is to do it in a way
that's blatant, but also interesting. For example, an email like this:
Subject Line: How to rub your belly fat away
Hey hey!
So I 	got this great question:
"___ , 	how is it possible to rub your belly fat away? I 	saw one of
your emails where you said your __ book shows us
how, 	but I 	just don't see how it's possible."
Oh it's possible.
In fact, 	it's based on Ancient Chinese techniques.
Basically it has to do with heat.
When you rub your belly the way I describe inside the book, 	the

See how that works?
It's a PURE and BLATANT sales pitch, full of tease and no meat whatsoever. It's
the kind of email that will send the time wasters and freebies seekers scurrying into the
dark like rats ... while making REAL customers (people serious about solving their
problem) want to buy. Plus, it is just interesting enough to keep the procrastinators sticking
around who might buy later, but would normally be turned off by too many blatant sales
pitches.
Yep, it's a FINE line to walk, no doubt.
But the more you practice these kinds of emails the easier it gets.
And, the more of the green stuff they will make you.

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
Take This Test
To See If You're
A Winner Or A Loser
Last summer I invented an email format that works like crazy for positioning your
product as "must have" for any serious prospect on the fence about buying from you or
someone else. I call it the "Winners and losers" email. And essentially, you combine the
time-tested (and proven) sales letter headline formula "Take This Test. .. " with the contrast
emails I've talked about before in the pages of this newsletter. I have been sitting here
trying to think of a way of explaining it. But, as with many things in life, a picture is
worth more than a 1,000 words. So here is a snapshot of a very responsive email using
this formula. I think you will find it quite easy to adapt and model (NOT use word-for-
word outright) to whatever you're selling.
Anyway, it's one of the most effective formats I've ever done:
Subject Line: Take this test to see if you're a winner or a loser
If you got the ballz, 	I 	dare you to take this test:
R,ead the following 	"winner/loser" thinking list, 	and honestly
assess what your answer would be 	(i.e. what your current beliefs
are about email marketing).
If you get too many 	(or ANY) 	"loser" answers, 	don't fret.
And don't even tell anyone.
(It's nobody else's business.)
Just change your wicked ways by doing what the winner would do,
and watch the money flow like lies from a goo-roo's lips.
Hit it, 	maestro:
The loser says "The more emails I 	send, 	the more unsubscribes I
get"

Like I said, I found this to be a very powerful format when done correctly. It
combines a lot of cool sales psychology --like repetition (which is very easy to read). It
challenges them. (Who wants to be a loser? Thus the reader starts thinking of ways to not
be a loser if he is doing any of the loser activities in the email). And the whole "take this
test" theme which has always been effective in space ads, etc.
It's easy to do, too.
Just make a list of all the things your prospects are doing wrong (and chances are
most will be committing at least one or two of the "sins" you describe, if not more ...) and
put them in this winner/loser format exactly as laid out in the above email. I don't have a
crystal ball (and I don't know of anyone else who's done this so have no other experiences
to draw upon), but methinks you'll get a good response.
So give it a shot.
Mter all, only losers never try...

-- 11 of 34 --

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
Gary Halbert's
''Sh3.11leful'' Em.ail Secret
If you read much of the late Gary Halbert's old newsletters, you will see he was
NOT a big fan of selling online. As much as I respect and admire his work, when it came
to the Internet he was pretty clueless (i.e. making people only order by phone online, etc).
And while I never met the man, I would suspect he'd find my using his famous '~ Pile/B
Pile" teaching in email to be almost... shameful -- like a little bit of leaven leavening the
whole lump of bread, and making it impure. But, use it I shall. Because, in many ways,
his teaching is even MORE powerful for email than it is direct mail. But before we apply
it to email, let's quickly go over what I'm talking about (in case you've never heard it).
Basically, it was Gary's opinion (and he was right) that people sort their mail over a waste
basket into two piles-- an 'W.' pile, and a "B" pile. The 'W.' pile being mail you HAVE to
open. Like bills, correspondence from the government and, yes, PERSONAL mail from a
friend or family member. These you absolutely will not throw out under any
circumstances without at least opening them up and seeing what's in there. Then there's
the "B" pile -- the obvious junk mail stuff. You know, mail that is slathered up with pretty
graphics, etc. Obvious commercial mail. The kind lazy postal workers are likely tempted
to burn if given the chance to lighten the load. Anyway, Gary was very insistent that
people make their direct mail ads look like 'W.' pile mail for the obvious reason that it is
far more likely to get opened and, thus, at least glanced at (and read and bought from)
than commercial looking mail that is obvious advertising at a glance.
Okay, so what's this got to do with email, exacdy?
Well, in my humble (but arrogant) opinion, it's even MORE important.
Email is deleted even faster than junk snail mail is tossed -- especially when
someone has 50-60 spam messages piled up, they ain't exacdy stopping to smell the roses
if you know what I mean. SO... because of this, it behooves us emailers to make our
email look as much like 'W.' pile email (i.e. emails that have to be at least opened and
glanced at) than "B" pile if we want the best chance of people reading our stuff. Below is
a list of things to think about when it comes to making your email like 'W.' pile, instead of
"B" pile. Some of them will seem like small potatoes. But it pays to be paranoid when it
comes to this stuff. Like Gary said in his teaching, pretend you HAD to get your message
-12-
www.BcnScttlc.com 	• 	Copyright 20 13 Ben Settle, All Rights Reserved 	• 	Published By Settle, LLC
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 34 --

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
read and bought from or you'd be beheaded. What would you do? How would you
approach it? So here are some ways to make your emails look 'W.' pile at a glance. They
don't guarantee 100% open rates obviously. But if you do all these things, you may find
over time you get significantly more sales (I know I have the more of these I
implemented).
Here we go ...
• 	Make the "From." Hne a na.Dle, and not a com.pany. So for example, having
the from line say "Ben Settle", not "Settle, LLC" or whatever
• 	Have the "From." em.ail address look like a person's real em.ail
address. i.e. "ben@bensettle.com" instead of "list@bensettle.com
• 	Plain old "retro" text. Yes, I know people say you get more clickthrus, etc with
HTML, but what about sales? What's more likely to look -- at a glance -- like it
COULD be personal? Plain text or pretty, neatly designed HTML template? At
the very least, make your HTML look plain text. And if you track it, track SALES,
not stoopid clickthrus. (After all, even spammers get high clickthrus sometimes,
doesn't mean they get high sales...)
• 	Non-shortened Hnes. I have no idea if this ever increased my sales, but it didn't
hurt them, and how many people-- outside of IM'ers at least-- send emails to
mom or dad in artificially shortened 70 character lines? I do again nowadays, but
that's mostly for faster writing. Try them both, see what happens
• 	Personal looking salutation. If I use a salutation at all, I'll say something like
"hey" or something really informal sounding -- like I'm sending it to a friend
• 	Misspellings. Yeah the spelling Nazis will freak, but who cares, a misspelling or
two won't hurt you. Old school mail order guys used to purposely misspell words
to look more "human" and less perfect, like the letter was just dashed off the
typewriter. Email is even MORE informal, misspellings, etc are not only forgiven
but pretty much ignored by most people, depending on who you're writing to
• 	Conversational tone. This isn't something anyone reading this newsletter
doesn't already know. But figured it should be included just in case...
-13-
www.BenSettle.com 	• 	Copyright 2013 Ben Settle, All Rights ReseiVed 	• 	Published By Settle, LLC

-- 13 of 34 --

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
• 	Not capitalizing every word in the subject lines. Again, do you capitalize
each word in your subject line when writing to Aunt Susie? Most people don't (if
they bother to put a subject line in at all)
Anyway, those are some things to play with.
The idea is to make it look at a glance your email is a personal, one-on-one email to
your reader. Just that split second where they think it MIGHT be personal can be enough
to get it opened. And if you do your job right, you can suck them in from there.
Again, it PAYS to be paranoid with email ...
A Secret Way To Use
The Electric Company
To Make More Sales Via Email
Okay this tip is GREAT for ending an auto-responder sequence.
Especially for nabbing people who have stayed until the very end of your sequence
and are on the fence and just need that little extra "push" to get over the hump and buy.
It's called the "Disconnection Notice" email and if you are on the right lists, you will see
this in direct mail all the time. The idea was (I am guessing) inspired by the electric
company at one time. You know, if you don't pay your bill they send you a nice little
disconnection notice letter telling you your lights are about to be turned off.
Kinda hard to ignore a letter like that, yeah?
Well, it's easily applied to email.
What I do is make the last email in the sequence a "disconnection notice" type
thing, telling them this is their last chance to buy, you won't hear from me again, and just
to try one more time, here's a discount on the product (and I usually make it a BIG
discount-- as the whole point is to get as many people from your prospect list to your
-14-

www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 14 of 34 --

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
buyers list as possible).
Again, think up a good irresistible offer (like for the special sales we talked about).
What can you offer them they MUST have? Then, put it in a disconnection email like
this (there is no offer, that's because it was for a client and I had no control over it):
Subject Line: 	Disconnection notice
{!firstname_fix},
Well, 	this is it.
You've been getting my free PRO tips ezine for months now.
I've sent you dozens and dozens of wealth-building and business
tips used by the world's richest and most successful
entrepreneurs. 	But clearly, 	you are not interested in taking
action on seeing if you even qualify for PRO or not.
And that's a shame.
The world can USE more good entrepreneurs now.
And the fact you read THIS far, 	means you have that fire in you.
Whatever the case, 	I'm going to disconnect you from getting this
ezine anymore. 	It's nothing personal, 	but if you're not an
action-taker, 	then PRO would not be right for you anyway 	(and if
you haven't taken action by now, 	let's face it, 	you probably
never will).
If you want to take one last look at PRO, 	you can here:
http://{!custom url}/thankyou{lcustom tracking}
Hopefully I'll see you on the PRO side.
But if not, 	good luck and remember to always go for the gold.
It's one of the few real thrills left in life today.
Jay Kubassek
World leader in home business
training & development
-15-
www.BenSettle.com 	• 	Copyright 2013 Ben Settle, All Rights Reserved 	• 	Published By Settle, LLC
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 15 of 34 --

THE 	EMAIL PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
Pretty easy stuff you can easily model for your own offers.
I like to do this in any auto-responder sequence I write.
Even if the "offer" is something as simple as giving them a coupon code for your
shopping cart for a huge discount of 50, 60, 70 or even 90% off. The whole point of the
first sale is not to necessarily make a profit, it's to build a list of buyers. I remember in one
info publishing business I did emails and copywriting for, they would even give the
product away for $1 just to get the person on the buyer's list. Because they knew what all
top direct marketers know, that a name of a buyer is infinitely more valuable than a
prospect.
###
Alrighty then, that's it for this month. Your assignment is to run a special sale as
described in here, count all the extra profits, and then send me a 10% cut (ha). Until next
time ...
Word up,
Ben Settle

The $20 copywriting seminar that saved my business
I 	ever tell you about my "crash course " in copywriting?
Basically, 	it went down like this:
For better or worse, 	other than little odd copywriting jobs here
and there 	(that paid little or no money) 	I 	got my real education by
taking on copywriting assignments where I 	got paid ONLY on
commission.
No freelancing fees.
Nothing up front.
Either my ads made money . .. or I was out a bunch of time, 	energy
and resources on my end .
Kind of stressful, 	to say the least.
Especially since, 	I 	only had a few copywriting resources.
Plus, 	time for studying the craft was l imited. 	I was working a 	job
at the time, was helping my wife in a small office cleaning
business we had, 	and my only time to study was in the car while
driving to and from work and on my lunch breaks .
What did I 	listen to?
Was it the "usual suspect " crop of goo-roos?
Hardly.
I 	didn ' t 	have time for nonsense.

=====
Was Gene Schwartz a copywriting sex fanatic?
Behold •••
My all-time favorite Eugene Schwartz headline:
"Should Any Man Be Given
This Much Sexual Power over
Women?"
This baby was on the outer envelope.
And it pulled MUCHO orders, 	too .
Here are a few 	(of many) 	reasons why:
* Isn't "salesy" 	(doesn't make your guard go up)
* Question 	(it's almost neurologically impossible to ignore a
question you want to know the answer to)
* Short 	( easy to process)
*Curiosity provoking (I mean, 	c•mon •• • )

Anyway, 	I 	got to thinking about that speech again today.
And specifically, 	a 	little piece of headline advice in it that
changed everything for me, 	and helped me pen winners even when my
ads were weak.
(Especially when I 	was 	just starting out.)
What was the advice?
Simply this •••
"When you are dealing with someone of real brilliance that does a
headline absolutely beyond all belief, then you are going to have a
hard time winning. 	Probably you are going to lose then no matter
how many facts you have."
In other words ...
It's ALL about the headline.
Not super writing & persuasion skillz.
Not sales choke holds and "fireworks."
And not even an irresistible offer.
Without that magic headline, 	even "great" copy will get slaughtered
by a newbie who writes a better one. Good news, 	for newbies, 	eh?
And maybe that's why he spends so much time on headlines in the
speech.

Where was I?
Oh yeah, 	Schwartz's testing advice.
And he wasn ' t 	just whistlin' 	dixie, 	either .
case in point:
While back I wrote a 	fartload of squeeze pages for a client. 	Not
just 2 or 3 test panels . •• 	but 20-30 test panels. 	Some of t hem play
it "safe" 	(not veering too much from their current control) 	and
some are so off the charts crazy different, 	they probably thought I
was a nutcase for even submitting them.
But you know what?
I 	fully expected 95% of those tests to FAIL.
To me they are all dead ads walking to that great electric chair of
cyberspace where probably only one of them will survive the
onslaught.
In fact, 	my first test failed by a 	fat 20% .
And that ' s good!
Did not bother me one bit.
I 	knew it ' d either win big or lose big.
And that's really the name of the game.
I mean, what would you rather have - - 	100 "play it safe" tests that
add up to a 	100% increase over weeks or months . • . 	or one BIG winner
that beats your control by 100% in a day?
Which do you think will make you money faster?
Something to think about.
To paraphrase one of the great American minds:
"Failure is COOL, 	Beavis."

I'm NOT a prophet, 	a 	fortune-teller or even Miss Cleo-- but it's

become even more relevant by year ' s end (where I ' ll make more
predictions for NEXT year, 	of course ••. )

Credibility will be KING.

whatsoever ••• 	just taking the time to put SOMETHING credible in
your ads should make you stand out like a 	fart in study hall .
